title: The Scientific Revolution
summary: A transformation in how European thinkers understood and investigated the natural world — running from Copernicus's heliocentric model of the solar system to Newton's laws of motion — that established the experimental method, mathematical description of nature, and the idea of a law-governed universe as the foundations of modern science.
hook: Between 1543 and 1687, European thinkers overturned a cosmology that had governed educated thought for nearly two thousand years. The Earth was displaced from the center of the universe; the heavens were shown to follow the same physical laws as the Earth; and the idea that nature could be precisely described in mathematical terms was demonstrated so convincingly that it has never since been seriously challenged. The Scientific Revolution is not merely a chapter in the history of science — it is the event that created modern science as a form of knowledge and established its authority over other ways of understanding the world.

beginner.theMovement: The Scientific Revolution was a transformation in how European thinkers understood and studied the natural world, taking place roughly between 1543 (when Copernicus published his heliocentric model of the solar system) and 1687 (when Newton published his Principia Mathematica, which explained the motion of planets and objects on Earth using the same mathematical laws). During this period, a series of thinkers — Copernicus, Tycho Brahe, Galileo, Kepler, Harvey, Boyle, Descartes, and Newton — overturned the ancient Greek cosmology that had dominated educated thought for nearly two thousand years and replaced it with a new understanding of nature based on observation, experiment, and mathematical description.
beginner.theOrigins: The movement had several origins. One was the recovery and translation of ancient Greek texts during the Renaissance, which gave European scholars access to the full range of Greek natural philosophy — including Archimedes's mathematical methods and Ptolemy's detailed astronomical models — rather than the filtered and interpreted versions that had been available through medieval scholarship. Another was the development of new instruments: the telescope (c. 1608), which made visible the moons of Jupiter and the phases of Venus; the microscope; and more precise astronomical instruments. A third was the growth of practical, craft-based knowledge — navigation, mining, military engineering — that created demand for more accurate and reliable descriptions of natural phenomena than ancient authorities could provide.
beginner.howItOrganized: The Scientific Revolution did not have formal institutions in its early phases — the key figures worked largely independently, in correspondence with each other and in competition. Galileo worked in Italian universities and courts; Kepler worked for the Habsburg emperor as an imperial mathematician; Descartes spent much of his career in the Netherlands. The movement began to institutionalize in the second half of the 17th century: the Royal Society of London (founded 1660) and the Académie Royale des Sciences in Paris (founded 1666) provided organizational frameworks for the exchange and verification of scientific results. The printing press was essential: it allowed the rapid dissemination of new findings across Europe and created the possibility of a scientific community that transcended national boundaries.
beginner.whatItAchieved: The movement's achievements were cumulative rather than produced by any single breakthrough. Copernicus proposed the heliocentric model but could not explain how or why the planets moved. Tycho Brahe accumulated the most precise astronomical observations made before the telescope. Kepler used Tycho's data to derive three mathematical laws of planetary motion. Galileo demonstrated that falling bodies accelerate at a constant rate regardless of their weight, overturning Aristotelian physics, and used the telescope to reveal a universe full of moons, craters, and sunspots that the ancient cosmology had not anticipated. Harvey demonstrated that blood circulates through the body — driven by the heart — overturning ancient medicine. Newton synthesized all of this in the Principia: three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation that could explain both the motion of planets and the fall of an apple.
beginner.contestedLegacy: The Scientific Revolution's legacy is contested in several directions. Within the history of science, the question of whether it represents a genuine 'revolution' — a sharp break with the past — or a more gradual development has been extensively debated since Thomas Kuhn's The Structure of Scientific Revolutions (1962). Within cultural history, the revolution has been read as liberating (establishing reason's authority over superstition) and as alienating (producing a 'disenchanted' world from which meaning has been removed). Within religious history, the conflict between the new science and religious authority — most dramatically in Galileo's trial before the Inquisition in 1633 — has been read as a founding moment of the conflict between science and religion, though most historians now argue this reading is substantially simplified.
beginner.whyItEnded: The 'Scientific Revolution' as a historical category is somewhat artificial — it is a retrospective label applied by historians to a series of developments that participants described in their own terms. The period it covers ended not because the revolution was complete but because its key achievements had been made and the institutional framework for subsequent science — universities, learned societies, scientific journals — had been established. What followed was not the end of scientific development but its normalization: the application of the new methods to a widening range of phenomena, producing the sciences of chemistry, electricity, and biology in the 18th century.

general.theMovement: The concept of a 'Scientific Revolution' was largely a creation of 20th-century historians of science, applied retrospectively to a series of developments in natural philosophy between the mid-16th and late 17th centuries. The participants did not use the term — they spoke of the 'new philosophy' or the 'new science' — and the unity of the period as a single 'revolution' has been contested from multiple directions. David Wootton's The Invention of Science (2015) defends the concept robustly, arguing that the period did produce a genuine transformation in what 'knowledge' meant and how it was produced. Steven Shapin's The Scientific Revolution (1996) begins with the provocative claim that 'there was no such thing as the Scientific Revolution' — meaning not that the developments didn't happen but that their unity as a single coherent event is a retrospective construction. Both positions illuminate real features of the period.
general.theOrigins: The origins of the Scientific Revolution cannot be reduced to a single cause. The Renaissance recovery of Greek texts provided both models (Archimedes's mathematical approach to physics) and problems (Ptolemy's geocentric model was precise enough to generate the anomalies that Copernicus tried to resolve). The rise of practical, craft-based knowledge in mining, metallurgy, navigation, and military engineering created demand for more reliable natural knowledge and brought educated scholars into contact with artisanal traditions of empirical testing. The Reformation's fragmentation of religious authority may have weakened the intellectual authority of the Catholic Church and the Aristotelian philosophy it had endorsed — though the relationship between Reformation and scientific change is debated. And the specific social and economic structure of Renaissance Italian courts, which provided patronage for intellectual innovation outside university curricula, allowed figures like Galileo to pursue research programs that university employment would not have supported.
general.howItOrganized: The social organization of the Scientific Revolution changed substantially across the period. In the early phase, natural philosophy was practiced primarily by university professors (though the most innovative work often happened outside university constraints), court intellectuals, and independent gentlemen of means. The correspondence network — letters circulating between figures like Mersenne in Paris, who maintained contact with virtually every major natural philosopher in Europe — was the primary mechanism for the dissemination and discussion of new ideas before the establishment of scientific societies and journals. The founding of the Royal Society (1660) and the Académie Royale des Sciences (1666) represented a new model: institutions dedicated to collective experimental inquiry, with the authority to certify results and regulate controversy. The first scientific journals — the Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society (1665) and the Journal des Sçavans (1665) — created the infrastructure for the modern scientific communication system.
general.whatItAchieved: The revolution's most important achievement was not any specific discovery but the establishment of a new epistemological standard: the idea that natural knowledge must be based on systematic observation and experiment, capable of mathematical description, and verifiable by others. This standard was articulated differently by different figures — Bacon emphasized induction from experiment, Descartes emphasized mathematical deduction from clear and distinct ideas, Newton combined both in his practice while refusing to commit to the underlying metaphysics ('hypotheses non fingo') — but all three approaches shared the rejection of Aristotelian authority as sufficient warrant for natural knowledge claims. The institutionalization of this standard through learned societies and journals created the social infrastructure within which the norm could be enforced.
general.contestedLegacy: The most important ongoing debate concerns the relationship between the Scientific Revolution and Christianity. The 'conflict thesis' — that science and religion are inherently in conflict, with the trial of Galileo as its founding moment — was a 19th-century construction (associated with John William Draper and Andrew Dickson White) that has been comprehensively rejected by historians of science. The reality is more complex: most of the key figures of the Scientific Revolution were sincere Christians who understood their work as revealing the mathematical design of God's creation. Newton spent more time on Biblical interpretation than on natural philosophy. The trial of Galileo was a specific political and theological confrontation that cannot be generalized into a structural conflict between science and religion. The Scientific Revolution did, however, establish the authority of a particular kind of knowledge — empirical, mathematical, verifiable — that subsequently made religious claims of empirical fact (about creation, miracles, the age of the Earth) increasingly difficult to maintain without revision.
general.whyItEnded: The concept of the Scientific Revolution 'ending' with Newton is a narrative convenience that historians have questioned. Newton did not resolve all the questions his work raised — the nature of gravity ('action at a distance' was philosophically troubling), the constitution of matter, the relationship between mechanics and life — and the 18th century saw the development of entirely new scientific fields (chemistry, electricity, geology, biology) using the methods the revolution had established. The better framing is that the Scientific Revolution established a set of institutional and epistemological norms that made subsequent scientific development both cumulative and self-correcting — the norms of modern science, which have been continuously applied and periodically revised since.

advanced.theMovement: The historiography of the Scientific Revolution has been substantially reshaped by three developments since the mid-20th century. The first was Thomas Kuhn's The Structure of Scientific Revolutions (1962), which introduced the concept of the 'paradigm shift' and argued that scientific revolutions are sociological as well as intellectual events — changes in the community of practitioners and their shared assumptions, not merely the accumulation of new facts. The second was the sociological turn in history of science associated with the Edinburgh School's 'strong programme,' which argued that scientific knowledge should be explained by the same social and political factors as any other form of belief. The third was the recovery of non-elite and non-institutional dimensions of early modern natural knowledge — craft traditions, women's participation in natural philosophy (Margaret Cavendish, for example), and the contribution of non-European knowledge traditions to European science through colonial encounter.
advanced.theOrigins: The relationship between the Scientific Revolution and the broader cultural changes of the Renaissance and Reformation has been extensively debated. The 'externalist' tradition in history of science, associated with Boris Hessen's 1931 argument that Newton's physics was shaped by the specific economic needs of early capitalism (navigation, mining, ballistics), argued that scientific change is driven by social and economic forces. The 'internalist' tradition argued that scientific ideas have their own internal logic of development. The contemporary consensus is more nuanced: neither pure internalism (ideas developing solely from other ideas) nor pure externalism (ideas determined by social forces) is adequate; the most productive work traces the specific mechanisms through which social contexts, intellectual traditions, and material practices interacted.
advanced.howItOrganized: Steven Shapin and Simon Schaffer's Leviathan and the Air-Pump (1985) is the most influential work on the social organization of early modern science. Their analysis of the dispute between Robert Boyle and Thomas Hobbes over the air-pump and the existence of a vacuum showed that the establishment of experimental facts as facts required not just the performance of experiments but a set of social conventions — who was allowed to witness, what counted as a valid observation, how disputes were to be resolved — that were politically contestable. Their key concept was 'matters of fact': the specific category of empirical claim that experimental philosophy produced, which was strategically valuable precisely because it claimed to be politically neutral (not committed to any particular metaphysical or religious position) while being socially produced through specific exclusionary practices.
advanced.whatItAchieved: The question of what 'mathematics' meant to the figures of the Scientific Revolution has been substantially refined by recent scholarship. 'Mathematization of nature' — the idea that the revolution replaced qualitative with quantitative descriptions — is too simple. Galileo's mathematization operated through idealization (the behavior of bodies in a frictionless vacuum, which doesn't exist) rather than direct measurement. Newton's laws are mathematical in form but their application to specific physical situations required substantial experimental and observational input. H. Floris Cohen's How Modern Science Came into the World (2010) provides the most comprehensive recent account of the different forms of mathematization operative in the period, distinguishing between the Archimedean tradition (mathematical description of static structures), the Galilean tradition (mathematical description of motion), and the Baconian tradition (systematic experimental accumulation of natural history).
advanced.contestedLegacy: The most consequential long-term effect of the Scientific Revolution may have been epistemological rather than substantive — not the specific discoveries made, but the establishment of a new standard for what counts as knowledge. Max Weber's concept of Entzauberung (disenchantment) — the progressive removal of magical, religious, and teleological explanations from the description of nature — captures one dimension of this transformation. Charles Taylor's A Secular Age (2007) traces the cultural consequences of the disenchanted worldview: a world without intrinsic meaning, in which values must be constructed by human beings rather than discovered in nature. The relationship between the Scientific Revolution and the subsequent history of secularization in Western societies is not simple — many scientists have remained deeply religious — but the authority of scientific knowledge over empirical questions has progressively limited the scope of religious claims.
advanced.whyItEnded: Peter Dear's Revolutionizing the Sciences (2001) argues for a distinction between two different projects within the Scientific Revolution: natural philosophy (understanding why nature behaves as it does) and what he calls 'science' in the modern sense (establishing reliable, instrumentally useful knowledge of how nature behaves, without necessarily explaining why). The Scientific Revolution established the authority of the second project over the first — producing reliable, mathematically described, experimentally verified knowledge of natural regularities — while leaving the deeper explanatory questions (what is gravity, what is light, what is life) open for subsequent investigation. This is why 'it ended': not because the questions were answered, but because the productive research program was established.

foundation.theStory: The Scientific Revolution transformed how European thinkers understood and studied the natural world, replacing a cosmology based on Aristotle and Ptolemy with one based on observation, experiment, and mathematics. Its key figures — Copernicus, Galileo, Kepler, Harvey, Boyle, Newton — overturned ancient authorities and established the methods that define modern science. It is the founding event of modern scientific knowledge.
foundation.theDebate: The central historical debate concerns whether the Scientific Revolution was as revolutionary as its name suggests — a sharp break with the past — or a more gradual development from medieval and Renaissance natural philosophy. The central philosophical debate concerns what the revolution actually changed: was it primarily about new facts, new methods, new institutions, or a new conception of what 'knowledge' means?
foundation.whyItMatters: The Scientific Revolution matters because it established the epistemological authority that science has maintained ever since. The idea that natural knowledge must be based on observation and experiment, capable of mathematical description, and verifiable by anyone — not on the authority of ancient texts or religious tradition — has been the defining feature of modern science and, increasingly, of knowledge generally. Understanding how this authority was established historically is essential for thinking critically about what science can and cannot do.
questions: The Ptolemaic geocentric system was not simply wrong — it was a sophisticated mathematical model that could predict planetary positions with reasonable accuracy. Why, then, was the Copernican heliocentric model accepted, given that it initially made no more accurate predictions? What does this tell you about how scientific revolutions actually work — and about the relationship between accuracy, simplicity, and the acceptance of new theories?

Francis Bacon argued that natural philosophy should be organized collectively and institutionally — that individual genius was less important than systematic, organized investigation. Newton worked largely alone and refused to share his methods. Whose model has proven more productive in the long run? What does modern 'big science' (particle physics, genomics, climate science) tell us about this question?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The most influential framework for understanding the Scientific Revolution remains Thomas Kuhn's concept of the paradigm shift — the idea that scientific revolutions involve not just new facts but a wholesale reorganization of what questions are worth asking and what counts as an answer. Kuhn's framework has been criticized for making scientific change seem irrational (if paradigm shifts are driven by social factors rather than evidence, how can science be objective?) and revised extensively. The most productive recent work combines attention to intellectual content (what specific claims were made and argued for) with attention to social and institutional context (who made them, in what settings, under what constraints).  Steven Shapin's work has been particularly important in showing that even apparently 'purely scientific' questions — what counts as a fact, who is allowed to verify a claim — are embedded in social structures.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidentiary situation for the Scientific Revolution is relatively good — the key texts survive, have been edited, and are available in translation. The main limitations are in recovering the experience of figures outside the educated male elite: women natural philosophers (Margaret Cavendish, Maria Sibylla Merian), artisanal practitioners whose craft knowledge contributed to the revolution, and non-European knowledge traditions whose contributions to European science are increasingly being traced. The history of what was lost as well as gained in the Scientific Revolution — indigenous knowledge systems that were dismissed or destroyed, non-mathematical ways of understanding nature that were marginalized — is being recovered by historians of science working in postcolonial frameworks.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible position on the Scientific Revolution must hold two things simultaneously: that it did produce genuine and important knowledge — the heliocentric solar system, the circulation of the blood, the laws of motion are real discoveries — and that the process by which this knowledge was produced was social, political, and culturally specific, not simply the neutral application of 'the scientific method.' The revolution's achievements were real; the idea that they are the product of a timeless, culturally neutral method is a retrospective mythology. This does not make science arbitrary, but it does make its history more interesting.
questions: Galileo was tried by the Inquisition in 1633 for supporting the Copernican heliocentric model. The standard narrative presents this as a case of religious authority suppressing scientific truth. The historical reality is more complex: Galileo had been permitted to discuss heliocentrism hypothetically, had supporters within the Church, and was convicted partly because he insulted the Pope. How does the more complex historical account change what the Galileo affair tells us about the relationship between science and religion?

The Scientific Revolution is sometimes credited with producing the idea that nature is a 'machine' governed by mathematical laws — and sometimes blamed for producing a disenchanted worldview that strips nature of meaning. Do you find the 'machine' metaphor liberating or impoverishing? What is gained and what is lost when we describe the natural world in purely mathematical and mechanical terms?

research.topic: The Copernican revolution and the reception of heliocentrism
research.content: The process by which Copernicus's heliocentric model was received, debated, and eventually accepted has been studied in detail. Owen Gingerich's The Book Nobody Read (2004) traces the annotated copies of De Revolutionibus across Europe to show that the book was actually widely read and carefully studied. The key finding is that the initial reception separated the mathematical model (useful for calculating planetary positions) from the physical claim (the Earth actually moves), allowing astronomers to use Copernicus's mathematics without committing to his cosmology. The physical commitment came later, through Galileo's telescopic observations and Kepler's demonstration that elliptical orbits fit the data better than circular ones.

research.topic: The social and institutional organization of early modern science
research.content: Shapin and Schaffer's Leviathan and the Air-Pump (1985) established the central questions for the social history of early modern science: how were facts established, who was authorized to witness and certify experiments, and how were disputes resolved? The analysis of the Royal Society's social conventions — the requirement of gentlemanly witnessing, the norm of 'modest' experimental reports that avoided controversial theoretical claims — showed that the authority of experimental facts was socially produced rather than simply derived from nature. Subsequent work has extended this analysis to other settings: the Italian academies, the Parisian salons, the German courts.

research.topic: Non-European contributions to the Scientific Revolution
research.content: The assumption that the Scientific Revolution was purely a European achievement has been substantially complicated by recent scholarship. The transmission of Arabic mathematical and astronomical knowledge to Europe through translation movements was a precondition for the Copernican program. The navigational and botanical knowledge produced by European colonial encounters with Asia, the Americas, and Africa contributed to the development of natural history and chemistry. Londa Schiebinger's Plants and Empire (2004) and other works have traced the systematic incorporation (and systematic suppression) of indigenous botanical knowledge into European science. The full dimensions of this global dimension of the revolution are still being researched.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Liberation of Reason
comparativeNarrative.content: The dominant traditional narrative of the Scientific Revolution was what Herbert Butterfield called 'Whig history' — a narrative of progressive liberation, in which scientific reason gradually freed itself from the superstition and authority of the medieval Church. In this narrative, Copernicus displaced humanity from the center of the universe, Galileo demonstrated that the Bible was not a scientific text, and Newton provided the mathematical foundation for a rational universe without need of theological explanation. This narrative was enormously influential in shaping the self-understanding of the Enlightenment and of modern secular culture. Professional historians of science have substantially revised it: the conflict between science and religion was more complex and less inevitable than the narrative suggests, and the 'progress' it describes was neither linear nor universal.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Disenchantment of the World
comparativeNarrative.content: Max Weber's concept of Entzauberung — the progressive removal of magic, spirit, and purpose from the description of nature — frames the Scientific Revolution as a cultural catastrophe as much as a cognitive achievement. In this reading, the mechanistic worldview produced by the revolution — nature as a machine governed by mathematical laws, without intrinsic meaning or value — severed the connection between human beings and the natural world that had been central to virtually all pre-modern cultures. The philosopher Descartes's division of reality into mind (res cogitans) and matter (res extensa) is often cited as the philosophical expression of this disenchantment. Critiques from this perspective argue that the price of scientific progress was a loss of the human capacity to experience nature as meaningful — a loss with consequences that range from environmental destruction to existential alienation.

comparativeNarrative.name: Parallel Developments and Unequal Power
comparativeNarrative.content: The framing of a 'Scientific Revolution' as a European achievement obscures the existence of sophisticated natural philosophical traditions in the Islamic world, China, India, and elsewhere that were developing simultaneously. The Islamic scientific tradition had produced major advances in astronomy, mathematics, optics, and medicine between the 9th and 14th centuries that were direct preconditions for the European revolution. Chinese technology had led the world in several domains for centuries before being overtaken. The question of why Europe produced the specific form of scientific development that it did — rather than Islamic, Chinese, or Indian natural philosophy — has been one of the central puzzles in the history of science. The answers typically involve specific features of European institutions (universities, the printing press, patronage systems) and political context rather than any intrinsic cultural superiority.

rabbitHole.label: The Printing Press
rabbitHole.reason: The dissemination of new scientific findings across Europe required the printing press — the Scientific Revolution's ideas could not have spread at the required speed without it

rabbitHole.label: The Enlightenment
rabbitHole.reason: The Enlightenment extended the Scientific Revolution's epistemological authority from natural philosophy to politics, morality, and society — applying reason to human affairs as Newton had applied it to nature

rabbitHole.label: On the Origin of Species
rabbitHole.reason: Darwin's theory of evolution applied the Scientific Revolution's methods to the question of life's history — extending the mechanical, law-governed universe to include biology

rabbitHole.label: Galileo
rabbitHole.reason: The figure whose work most dramatically embodies both the achievements and the conflicts of the Scientific Revolution — and whose trial is the most famous single episode in the history of science and religion

rabbitHole.label: Mathematics
rabbitHole.reason: Zero and positional notation were preconditions for the mathematical language in which Newton expressed his laws — the Scientific Revolution's discoveries were expressed in a mathematics that itself had a history

rabbitHole.label: Isaac Newton
rabbitHole.reason: The culminating figure of the Scientific Revolution — whose Principia Mathematica unified Kepler's planetary laws and Galileo's terrestrial mechanics under a single mathematical framework

rabbitHole.label: The Royal Society
rabbitHole.reason: The institutional expression of the Scientific Revolution — the first learned society dedicated to experimental natural philosophy, founded in London in 1660

rabbitHole.label: The Scientific Revolution — Steven Shapin (1996)
rabbitHole.reason: The best short introduction to the historiography as well as the history — Shapin's paradoxical opening sentence ('There was no such thing as the Scientific Revolution') announces the book's aim of questioning received narratives while presenting them

reference.author: David Wootton
reference.title: The Invention of Science: A New History of the Scientific Revolution
reference.annotation: The most comprehensive recent defense of the 'Scientific Revolution' as a genuine historical phenomenon — Wootton argues that the period produced genuinely new concepts (fact, experiment, theory, law of nature) that transformed what knowledge meant.

reference.author: Steven Shapin
reference.title: The Scientific Revolution
reference.annotation: The best short introduction to the historiography — begins provocatively ('There was no such thing as the Scientific Revolution') and delivers a sophisticated account of what was and was not new about the period's natural philosophy.

reference.author: Thomas Kuhn
reference.title: The Structure of Scientific Revolutions
reference.annotation: Not specifically about the Scientific Revolution but indispensable for understanding it — Kuhn's concept of the paradigm shift transformed how historians think about scientific change and generated a debate that is still ongoing.

reference.author: Peter Dear
reference.title: Revolutionizing the Sciences: European Knowledge and Its Ambitions, 1500-1700
reference.annotation: A clear, synthetic account of the period that distinguishes productively between different forms of natural inquiry and situates scientific change within broader cultural and institutional contexts.

commerce.title: The Scientific Revolution
commerce.author: Steven Shapin
commerce.note: The best short introduction — read it first

commerce.title: The Invention of Science
commerce.author: David Wootton
commerce.note: For readers who want the full argument — comprehensive and clearly written

commerce.title: Cosmos: A Spacetime Odyssey — Neil deGrasse Tyson (2014)
commerce.note: The documentary medium inevitably simplifies, but the Cosmos series communicates the excitement of the transformation in human understanding of the universe with unusual vividness

popularCulture.title: Life of Galileo — Bertolt Brecht (1943)
popularCulture.description: Brecht's play about Galileo's trial before the Inquisition was written during the Second World War and revised after Hiroshima — making it as much about the responsibility of scientists for the uses of their discoveries as about the historical confrontation between Galileo and the Church. Brecht's Galileo is heroic, cowardly, brilliant, self-indulgent, and ultimately ambiguous: a scientist who recants to save his life and continues working in secret, producing results whose consequences he cannot control. The play raises questions about science, power, and moral responsibility that have become more rather than less urgent since its composition.

popularCulture.title: Isaac Newton
popularCulture.description: Newton's career embodies several of the central paradoxes of the Scientific Revolution. He is the figure who most definitively established the mathematical law-governed universe — and yet he spent more time on alchemy and Biblical prophecy than on natural philosophy. The Newton of the Principia (rational, mathematical, definitive) and the Newton of the private papers (alchemist, millenarian, obsessive) are difficult to reconcile, and the reconciliation itself tells us something important: the boundary between 'science' and other forms of knowledge was not clear to Newton himself.

popularCulture.title: The Baroque Cycle — Neal Stephenson (2003-2004)
popularCulture.description: Stephenson's massive trilogy (Quicksilver, The Confusion, The System of the World) is set in the world of the late Scientific Revolution and Enlightenment, with Newton, Leibniz, and the Royal Society as characters alongside fictional protagonists. It is fiction, not history, but its imaginative reconstruction of the intellectual world of the period — the overlapping of alchemy, mathematics, theology, politics, and trade — captures something about the early modern scientific world that more conventional histories miss.
